images.caption: Figure 1:Validation loss versus computeCmC_{m}in the loss–CCview under LayerMix data with repetition. Curves are fit on 252M–1.2B and extrapolated to larger models. The traditional scaling law mis-extrapolates under repetition, while InfoLaw tracks both interpolation and extrapolation across recipes (HQ and MLQ).

(a)The fitted quality density functionfdf_{d}. The quality density is a monotonically decreasing function of the bucket index, meaning buckets with higher-quality data are assigned a higher density value.

(a)The fitted quality density functionfdf_{d}. The quality density is a monotonically decreasing function of the bucket index, meaning buckets with higher-quality data are assigned a higher density value.

(b)The relationship betweenλ\lambdaandNNwith a fitted curve. The blue scattered points represent the observed data. The solid red line shows the fit within the data range, while the dashed line represents the extrapolation.

Figure 4:Cross-Regime Prediction of the Scaling Law. The blue line (Cm′C_{m^{\prime}}) is a pure prediction, generated using parameters fitted only on theCmC_{m}data (black line). The fit for theCm′C_{m^{\prime}}points demonstrates our InfoLaw’s power to extrapolate across different overtrain degrees.

The logarithmic form provides the best in-domain fit and extrapolation behavior compared with the exponential and power-law alternatives. Solid lines denote interpolation over observedNN; dashed lines indicate extrapolation beyond the observed range.
